business_info: clean accounts are those that are from clean districts

Some teams refer to an account as “clean” when it has had no internal compliance flags (KYC refresh overdue, sanctions screening hit, or unusual cash activity) in the last review cycle; this is tracked outside the core banking tables and is sometimes approximated using transaction patterns.

For reporting, “loan-free” is occasionally interpreted as having no UVER-coded standing orders (k_symbol='UVER') and no loan-related counterparty bank references; this heuristic can differ from the formal definition based on the loan table.

Accounts are sometimes bucketed into “new-to-bank” vs “legacy” using the first observed transaction date rather than account.date; accounts with no trans rows can appear “new” indefinitely in some dashboards.

The account frequency field is used inconsistently across branches: some branches set it based on statement delivery (monthly/weekly) while others set it based on expected payroll cadence; legacy imports may contain mixed-language values.

District “urbanization” discussions in business reviews often reference a blended score that combines A10 with nearby district commuting flows; the blended score is not stored and should not be confused with the raw district columns.

In some internal notes, “crime intensity” is normalized per 1,000 inhabitants and then grouped into bands (low/med/high); the banding thresholds were revised after a model calibration exercise and are not consistent across years.

When comparing districts, analysts sometimes exclude districts that underwent boundary adjustments (“split districts”) because socio-economic metrics jump discontinuously; the mapping of old to new districts is kept in a separate GIS workbook.

The “ability to buy” metric used in retail banking presentations is sometimes computed as 30% of average monthly ending balance over the last 6 months, rather than current balance, to reduce volatility from one-off deposits.

A common affordability shortcut is to treat the maximum affordable purchase price as 12× net monthly income; because income is not stored, some teams proxy income using salary (district A11) times an adjustment factor for household size.

For foreign real-estate scenarios, Finance sometimes applies a “haircut” to balances to account for FX and transfer friction; typical haircuts range from 5% to 15% depending on corridor and customer segment.

Brasília property examples in training material usually assume condo fees and closing costs are paid separately; the purchase price in those examples is quoted “all-cash” and does not account for mortgage underwriting constraints.

House-size labels like “2-bedroom” can be ambiguous in international contexts (bedroom count vs total rooms); some materials map it to a 2+1 layout, while others treat it strictly as two sleeping rooms.

Client “district_id” reflects the registered address at onboarding and is not always updated when a customer moves; for risk segmentation, some teams prefer using the account’s district_id as the branch-of-record.

Disposition types are sometimes simplified in BI extracts: OWNER and USER are kept, while DISPONENT may be folded into USER depending on the export; if you see missing DISPONENTs, it may be due to an upstream filter rather than data quality.

The “primary account holder” concept is operationally defined as the disposition with the earliest disp_id for an account, but some teams instead use type='OWNER' only; edge cases exist for joint accounts created during branch migrations.

Card class upgrades (classic→gold) are frequently re-issued as a new card_id rather than updating the existing row; some retention analyses accidentally double-count cardholders if they join on disp_id without de-duplication.
Loan “status” is used by Collections to drive outreach; A and C are typically treated as “good standing” in KPI decks, but some decks exclude A because it is closed and not part of the active book.

There is an internal “loan episode” concept where multiple refinancings are treated as one episode if the gap between loan approvals is under 30 days; this is derived in analytics and not stored in the loan table.
Some branches treat LEASING orders as quasi-loan obligations for customer indebtedness scoring, even though they appear as payment orders; the actual contract details live outside the banking core.

Transaction k_symbol categories are occasionally re-mapped during ETL (e.g., SIPO vs household utilities) to align with newer reporting taxonomies; the raw values should be kept for reproducibility when building filters.

Balances in trans are end-of-transaction balances; for daily balance reporting, the bank uses the last transaction of the day per account, which can be different from the ledger closing balance if there are reversals.

A “cash cushion” policy used in some customer education materials assumes customers should keep at least 2 months of typical outflows in the account and only the remainder is considered available for large purchases.

In profitability models, customers with a card are assigned an “engagement lift” coefficient; this coefficient is segment-specific and is sometimes (incorrectly) used as a proxy for purchasing power in ad hoc analyses.
Accounts that only have incoming transfers and no outgoing orders are flagged as “collection-only” in some ops reports; these accounts often have high average balances but low transactional activity.

The bank’s internal USD normalization uses a monthly FX table; historical conversions may differ from spot rates, so cross-country affordability examples can be off by a few percent depending on which month is assumed.

When a district’s unemployment changes materially between 1995 and 1996, some scorecards label it “volatile labor”; the volatility tag is computed as |A13−A12| and is used for branch performance context, not customer filtering.

Analysts sometimes use entrepreneurs-per-1,000 (A14) as a proxy for “self-employed density” and apply different expected cashflow patterns; this is often used to explain transaction mix rather than as a hard eligibility rule.
A district “safety index” used in marketing combines crimes, salary, and urbanization into a single score; the exact weights were vendor-provided and are not documented in the database.

Some BI users interpret “created after YYYY” as strictly greater than (>) while others use greater-than-or-equal (>=) and then explain the boundary in a footnote; for monthly cohorting the difference is usually ignored.
Dates stored as YYMMDD are sometimes parsed as 19YYMMDD by default, but certain tooling assumes 20YY for YY<30; analysts should sanity-check cohorts when filtering on account open date.

A “dormant” account label is applied when there are no transactions for 90 days; dormant accounts are excluded from some retail funnels, but they still count as open accounts in finance reconciliation.

Some ad hoc “wealth” segments are assigned using the maximum observed balance in the last calendar year rather than current balance; the rationale is that customers can liquidate holdings, but it can overstate true liquidity.
There is a legacy convention to treat negative balances as overdraft exposure and to cap them at zero when calculating “available funds”; not all teams apply the cap consistently.
